text.title: Trend Lifecycle and STEEP Analysis
text.summary: Trend categories, adoption curve stages, entry timing rules, and the STEEP analysis framework.

list.item: Fad: weeks to months duration; no structural driver; avoid building a business on it
Trend: 2-5 year window; structural driver exists (technology, demographics, regulation); the solopreneur sweet spot
Megatrend: decade-scale; high certainty but slow to monetize; entry point is a specific sub-trend within it
Shift: permanent structural change; treat as the new baseline, not an opportunity in itself

section.title: Adoption Curve Stages and Entry Timing

list.item: Innovators: define the category; too early — no market exists yet, education cost dominates
Early Adopters: high margins but you educate the market for competitors; viable if you have a distribution advantage
Early Mainstream (optimal entry): market aware, solutions emerging, growth inflecting — enter here
Late Mainstream: volume available but commoditization accelerating; viable only with cost or distribution moat
Decline: shrinking market; enter only if you are serving a niche/legacy segment deliberately

rule.statement: Require a quantitative anchor for any "Early Mainstream" claim: category spend at top 3 players, named buyer logos, or revenue data. Reject the label if only qualitative signals support it.
rule.rationale: "Early Majority" labels are subjective and unfalsifiable without quantitative anchors. The same trend can look "early" or "late" depending on the analyst's framing and the segment chosen.

list.item: Social: behavior and value shifts (e.g., remote-work normalization, privacy expectations, demographic aging)
Technology: enabling tech and capability thresholds (e.g., GPT-4 function calling, WebAssembly, CRISPR)
Economic: financial forces (e.g., interest-rate cycle, labor cost arbitrage, VC dry powder cycles)
Environmental: sustainability factors and climate risk (e.g., carbon pricing, ESG mandates, supply-chain resilience)
Political: regulatory landscape and policy pipeline (e.g., EU AI Act, MiCA, GDPR, FTC enforcement trends)

rule.statement: Track the regulatory pipeline as a separate axis from STEEP-Political, with explicit stage (proposal / enacted / in-force) and effective date. Proposed rules are not yet constraints; enacted rules with a future effective date are the real driver.
rule.rationale: Regulatory stage confusion is a major agent failure mode. A proposed rule treated as in-force leads to premature product decisions; an enacted rule with a near effective date treated as "proposal" causes compliance misses.

list.item: Growth rate (25%): measured YoY from 3+ independent sources; reject single-source CAGR
Adoption curve stage (20%): based on quantitative anchor, not label
Number of well-funded incumbents (15%): proxy for competitive density
Investment activity (20%): funding velocity and deal count from Crunchbase/PitchBook
Personal fit + licensing (20%): skills, capital, and regulatory licence requirements

rule.statement: Apply the personal-fit filter BEFORE presenting a high-scoring trend as an opportunity. A regulated category (broker-dealer, healthcare, gambling) with a strength score of 5/5 is unreachable without the required license.
rule.rationale: A high trend-strength score in a licensed category is worthless without the license. Surfacing unlicensed opportunities wastes planning resources and creates false urgency.
